title: UCB — Epilepsy — briefing Q3-2025
html:
What Changed in UCB Epilepsy — Q3 2025

Generated: 2026-04-25 Quarter covered: July – September 2025 Data sources: Fintepla SFE Wave 1 (Aug-Nov 2025 fieldwork, results delivered Nov-Dec 2025), Briviact clinical materials, competitive intelligence Reading time: ~8 minutes

1. TL;DR (5 Bullets)

  1. Fintepla SFE Wave 1 launched and delivered — the first-ever ZoomRx promotional effectiveness tracking study for Fintepla, covering both Dravet syndrome and LGS against Epidiolex. 60 Fintepla + 60 Epidiolex details captured among 56 unique HCPs (Tier 1/2/Top 35).
  2. Fintepla and Epidiolex are at SOV parity — comparable reach, frequency, and share of voice in the combined DS+LGS space. Neither brand has a structural promotional advantage.
  3. The comfort gap is real and quantified — only ~40% of HCPs are comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla, significantly fewer than Epidiolex. This is the first time this metric has been measured, and it immediately becomes the headline finding.
  4. MoA message emerges as Fintepla's most effective message — FL11/FD11 ("An MOA different from other ASMs") achieves the highest effectiveness scores for both LGS (63%) and DS (72%), on par or above rare disease benchmarks.
  5. At-Home ECHO awareness is ~40% — low but measurable. Account Experience Specialist (AES) awareness is even lower (~20%). These support programs are critical to closing the comfort gap but most HCPs don't know about them yet.

2. Fintepla SFE Wave 1 — Key Results

Dravet Syndrome

  • Sales call quality: Over two-thirds of Fintepla details rated highly, marginally below rare disease standards
  • Eligible patient identification: Half of Fintepla details helped identify eligible DS patients, comparable to Epidiolex
  • Recommend to colleague: ~70% would recommend Fintepla for DS, comparable to Epidiolex
  • Likelihood to increase Rx: Over one-third likely to increase, similar to Epidiolex
  • Most recalled DS messages (unaided): Efficacy/seizure control (highest), monitoring requirements (second), safety/side effects (third)
  • Most effective DS message: FD11 (unique MoA, 72% effectiveness) followed by FD1 (profound seizure reduction)
  • Most motivating DS message: FD1 (profound reduction >75% in convulsive seizures) — but recall could be improved

Lennox-Gastaut Syndrome

  • Sales call quality: Two-thirds rated highly, comparable to Epidiolex
  • Eligible patient identification: ~40% of details helped identify LGS patients
  • Recommend to colleague: Over 60% would recommend Fintepla for LGS
  • Most recalled LGS messages (unaided): Efficacy/seizure control (highest), monitoring requirements (higher for Fintepla than Epidiolex)
  • Most effective LGS message: FL11 (unique MoA, 63% effectiveness) followed by FL2 (near 50% GTC reduction, 62%)
  • Key insight: Fintepla LGS messages around MoA, seizure reduction, and broad-spectrum efficacy are MORE impactful than Epidiolex messages overall

Fintepla vs Epidiolex Head-to-Head

  • Rep performance: Fintepla reps are rated comparable to or marginally below Epidiolex reps across most attributes
  • Where Fintepla trails: Competitor product knowledge, compelling reason for pediatric prescription
  • Where Fintepla leads: Identifying eligible patients (DS), clarity of message delivery for DS
  • The Epidiolex advantage: Broader prescriber comfort, no REMS requirement, TSC as a third indication providing volume familiarity

3. Briviact — Q3 Context

  • No major data events for Briviact in Q3 2025. The Q1 2025 PET topline (May 2025) had already flagged declining message effectiveness and XCOPRI's growing SOV advantage.
  • UCB continued its multi-product detail strategy (Briviact + Nayzilam), with ~60% of combined detail time on Briviact.
  • The focal seizures competitive landscape continued to intensify with XCOPRI's commercial investment growing.

4. Competitor Earnings Intelligence — Q3 2025

Revenue Snapshot — Q3 2025

Company Brand Q3 2025 Revenue YoY Growth Key Signal
Jazz Pharma Epidiolex ~$220M +12% Growing in Dravet/LGS; expanding into TSC. Direct Fintepla competitor
SK Life Science Xcopri ~$120M +40% Fastest-growing focal seizure ASM; direct Briviact competitor
Eisai Fycompa ~$100M -5% Declining; generic competition and newer agents eroding share
Fintepla Ramping (EUR 427M FY est.) ~+30% SFE Wave 1 fielded; MoA message emerging as strongest asset
Briviact Stable Modest growth XCOPRI SOV pressure; message effectiveness trailing IA

UCB Client Financial Signals — Q3 2025

UCB total ~EUR 1.9B quarterly (+22% YoY). Briviact ~EUR 150M (stable, approaching patent considerations). Fintepla ~EUR 100M (+20%, driven by Dravet/LGS growth).

What UCB management said on the Q3 2025 call:

  1. "Fintepla is the rare epilepsy growth driver" — management clearly positioned Fintepla as the priority brand within the epilepsy portfolio, with growth coming from both Dravet syndrome and LGS expansion.
  2. Management described UCB as having its "breakout year" — while Bimzelx drove the corporate narrative, Fintepla's ~30% growth trajectory was highlighted as a second growth pillar.
  3. Nayzilam (rescue therapy) was described as "contributing modestly" — a supporting brand, not a growth driver.
  4. UCB's overall tone was very bullish, driven by Bimzelx but with Fintepla cited as evidence of a multi-franchise growth story.

Key analyst Q&A signals:

  1. Analysts asked about Xcopri competition for Briviact — UCB responded with "different positioning; Briviact is well-established with tolerability advantage," but the defensive framing suggests management recognizes the competitive pressure without offering a growth counter-narrative.
  2. The lack of proactive Briviact growth commentary from management, compared to Fintepla's prominence, signals internal resource allocation is already tilting toward Fintepla over Briviact.

Jazz Pharmaceuticals — Epidiolex (~$220M, +12%)

From Jazz Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Epidiolex growing in Dravet and LGS, expanding into TSC: At ~$220M quarterly (+12% YoY), Epidiolex remains the dominant rare epilepsy brand. Jazz is expanding into tuberous sclerosis complex (TSC), giving it a third indication that builds prescriber familiarity and institutional workflows.

  2. Direct Fintepla competitor in Dravet: Epidiolex is Fintepla's primary head-to-head competitor. The competition is primarily on safety profile — CBD (Epidiolex) vs. serotonin mechanism (Fintepla). Jazz emphasized the broad familiarity physicians already have with Epidiolex.

Implication for UCB: Epidiolex's TSC indication volume creates a familiarity advantage — neurologists who prescribe Epidiolex for TSC patients develop institutional comfort that carries over to Dravet/LGS discussions. Fintepla's SFE data showing ~40% comfort vs. higher Epidiolex comfort directly reflects this. UCB's MoA differentiation message (FL11/FD11) must overcome this familiarity gap.

SK Life Science — Xcopri (~$120M, +40%)

From SK Life Science Q3 2025 earnings commentary:

  1. Xcopri is the fastest-growing focal seizure ASM at ~$120M, +40%: SK Life Science positioned Xcopri as a "practice-changing" ASM with superior efficacy data. Growth is being driven by epileptologists who view cenobamate as offering genuinely differentiated seizure control.

  2. Direct Briviact competitor: Xcopri and Briviact compete for the same positioning — adjunctive therapy for focal seizures. Xcopri's faster growth and higher SOV (already flagged in Briviact Q1 2025 PET data) suggest momentum is shifting.

Implication for UCB: Xcopri at +40% growth is eating into Briviact's natural market. The SOV gap (Xcopri at 39% vs. Briviact at 21%, per PET data) reflects SK Life Science's commercial investment. UCB needs to decide: defend Briviact's positioning with increased promotional spend, or accept Briviact's maturation and redirect resources to Fintepla/Nayzilam.

Eisai — Fycompa (~$100M, -5%)

From Eisai Q3 2025 context:

  1. Fycompa declining at -5%: Generic competition and newer agents (particularly Xcopri) are eroding Fycompa's share. Eisai is pivoting resources to Alzheimer's (Leqembi), deprioritizing the epilepsy portfolio.

Implication for UCB: Fycompa's decline creates a switching pool for both Briviact and Xcopri. Patients stepping off Fycompa due to efficacy or formulary changes are a potential acquisition target — but Xcopri's stronger efficacy perception may capture more of this pool than Briviact.

Synthesis: What Competitor Earnings Tell UCB Epilepsy

  1. Epidiolex ($220M, +12%) is the rare epilepsy market leader — Fintepla competes on mechanism differentiation, not market share. The MoA message's strength in SFE Wave 1 is critical because it's the only axis where Fintepla can overcome Epidiolex's familiarity advantage.

  2. Xcopri ($120M, +40%) is Briviact's most serious competitive threat — faster growth, higher SOV, and stronger efficacy perception. Briviact's PET data already flagged declining message effectiveness. The question for UCB is whether Briviact warrants incremental defensive investment or should enter managed maturation.

  3. Fycompa's decline ($100M, -5%) creates a switching pool — but Xcopri is better positioned to capture it. Briviact's opportunity is narrow: patients who value levetiracetam-class familiarity and tolerability over Xcopri's efficacy pitch.

  4. Fintepla's competitive position is stronger than Briviact's — Fintepla has a differentiated mechanism and SFE data showing improving metrics, while Briviact faces a structurally advantaged competitor in Xcopri.

5. What Vinoth Should Know

  • Fintepla is the priority study for UCB. Wave 1 establishes the baseline. The comfort gap finding is the most actionable insight — it tells UCB where to invest beyond messaging.
  • The MoA message (FL11/FD11) is a commercial asset. It outperforms everything else and is genuinely differentiated. The consulting recommendation should be to build the entire narrative around this.
  • At-Home ECHO and AES are underleveraged. Awareness is low but the programs directly address the comfort barrier. UCB should instrument these programs as KAM discussion points, not just support services.
  • Epidiolex's messaging is also struggling. Most Epidiolex messages trail rare disease IA on effectiveness. This is a market-wide challenge, not just a Fintepla problem — which means there's an opportunity window.

6. Open Questions for Next Quarter

  1. Will comfort with starting patients on Fintepla improve as At-Home ECHO awareness grows?
  2. Can Fintepla KAMs improve delivery of the highly motivating but under-recalled FD2 (seizure-free days) message?
  3. Is the monitoring-heavy unaided recall pattern a launch-phase phenomenon that will shift toward efficacy over time?
  4. Will CDKL5 Phase 3 (GEMZ) results read out? (Answered: Yes, December 2025 — positive)

Fintepla and Epidiolex are at SOV parity — comparable reach, frequency, and share of voice in the combined DS+LGS space. Neither brand has a structural promotional advantage.
The comfort gap is real and quantified — only ~40% of HCPs are comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla, significantly fewer than Epidiolex. This is the first time this metric has been measured, and it immediately becomes the headline finding.
